title: Canny Edge Detection
concept: Canny edge detection is a multi-step classical recipe — smooth first, measure gradients, thin the results, then threshold — that turns a noisy raw edge response into clean, meaningful outlines.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's a downside of using a raw Sobel filter by itself?

options.text: It can produce thick, noisy edges and reacts to things like shadows or texture, not just real boundaries
It runs too slowly to ever be useful
It only works on black-and-white photos

exercises.explain: Right — Sobel alone is noisy and imprecise. Canny builds a smarter pipeline on top of that same idea.

cards.title: Sobel's edges are fuzzy and thick
cards.body: Run a Sobel filter on a real photo and the "edges" often come out messy — thick, noisy, and littered with false alarms from shadows or texture. Professional computer vision pipelines needed something cleaner, so in 1986 engineer John Canny designed a smarter multi-step recipe.

cards.title: What is Canny edge detection?
cards.body: Canny edge detection is a classical algorithm that runs several steps in order: 1) smooth the image first to reduce noise, 2) measure gradients (brightness jumps, like Sobel does) to find where edges might be, 3) thin the thick edge blobs down to single crisp lines, and 4) threshold to keep only the strong, meaningful edges and drop weak noise.

cards.title: Editing an essay in passes
cards.body: Think of proofreading an essay in stages: first you clean up typos (smoothing), then you find where the argument actually shifts (gradients), then you trim rambling sentences down to the essential line (thinning), and finally you cut any weak, unconvincing points (thresholding). Canny cleans up edges the same way, one pass at a time.

cards.title: A street photo, before and after
cards.body: Point a raw Sobel filter at a busy street photo and you get thick, speckly edges everywhere — car outlines tangled up with texture and shadow noise. Run the same photo through the full Canny pipeline, and you get thin, clean, connected outlines of the cars and buildings, with the noisy false edges filtered out.

cards.title: Canny isn't a learned model
cards.body: It's easy to assume something this good at cleaning up messy edges must involve machine learning. It doesn't. Canny is a fixed, hand-designed classical algorithm from decades before modern deep learning — the same four steps run in the same order every time, no training or data required.

cards.title: Sobel alone vs. the full Canny pipeline
cards.body: One step vs. a full recipe:

left.text:
Thick, noisy edge blobs
Sensitive to shadows and texture

right.text:
Smoothed first, then thinned and thresholded
Thin, clean, meaningful edges

exercises.prompt: Which best describes the order of steps in Canny edge detection?

options.text: Smooth to reduce noise, find gradients, thin the edges, then threshold to keep only strong ones
Threshold first, then guess where edges might be afterward
Thin the edges before ever measuring any gradients
Only measure gradients — smoothing and thresholding are skipped entirely

exercises.explain: Correct — smoothing first prevents noise from corrupting the gradient step, and thinning plus thresholding clean up the result afterward.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Canny edge detection smooths the image before measuring brightness gradients.
exercises.explain: True. Smoothing first reduces noise so the gradient step doesn't mistake tiny fluctuations for real edges.

exercises.sentence: After finding gradients, Canny ___ the thick edge response down to single, crisp lines.
exercises.explain: Thins is correct — that step narrows fat edge blobs down to one-pixel-wide lines.

exercises.prompt: Which photo processing result best shows the benefit of full Canny edge detection over raw Sobel?

options.text: A street photo produces thin, clean outlines of cars and buildings, with shadow noise filtered out
A photo's colors become more vivid and saturated
A photo's file size shrinks significantly

exercises.explain: Clean, thin, noise-filtered outlines are exactly what the extra Canny steps add on top of raw Sobel.

exercises.prompt: Across blurring, sharpening, Sobel, and Canny, what do all of these classical filters have in common?

options.text: They all slide a small, fixed kernel across the image and do the same repeatable local math at every position — no learning involved
They all require a trained neural network to decide what each pixel should become
They only work on already-compressed JPEG files
They each require a human to manually edit every pixel by hand

exercises.explain: Exactly! Every filter here — blur, sharpen, Sobel, Canny — used a fixed, hand-designed kernel doing the same sliding-window math. Next up: Convolutional Neural Networks take this exact sliding-kernel idea and let the network learn its own kernel values automatically, instead of a human choosing the numbers.
